jokes.text: They are either gonna say... yes or no. Could go either way. We don't know what they are going to say.
jokes.setup: Michael is trying to think through a corporate decision logically
jokes.punchline: His profound insight is that they could say yes or no - the most obvious binary outcome possible
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael Scott pseudo-wisdom - he thinks he's being profound while stating the obvious. Character-specific humor that shows his management style.

jokes.text: I don't like to be cooped up in that office. In that box all day long. Heisman!
jokes.setup: Michael is explaining why he needs to get out of the office
jokes.punchline: He randomly shouts 'Heisman!' while presumably doing the pose during office football
jokes.explanation: Michael's juvenile excitement about office football, randomly calling out football moves. Fits his character but not particularly sophisticated.

jokes.text: Office football chaos with everyone demanding the ball from each other
jokes.setup: They're playing office football
jokes.punchline: Everyone frantically demanding the ball creates chaos, ending with Ryan getting hurt
jokes.explanation: Standard office chaos humor. The escalation works but it's fairly predictable physical comedy.

jokes.text: You are so lucky, Jim. You are so lucky you don't have this problem.
jokes.setup: Michael is looking at electronics deals
jokes.punchline: He envies Jim for not having money to buy things he wants
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Michael - he genuinely thinks being poor is lucky because you don't have to make purchasing decisions. Shows his privilege and obliviousness.

jokes.text: Question, do their pizzas play DVDs?
jokes.setup: Discussion of pizza as a prize
jokes.punchline: Michael asks if the pizzas can play DVDs, mixing up pizza with electronics
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist humor with perfect economy. The question is so unexpectedly ridiculous it catches you off-guard. Quintessential Michael Scott randomness.

jokes.text: It is literally the highest possible honor that a Northeastern Pennsylvania based mid-size paper company regional salesman can attain
jokes.setup: Jim describing Dwight's achievement
jokes.punchline: He makes it sound prestigious while simultaneously describing how incredibly specific and limited the achievement is
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim - he's both congratulating Dwight and subtly mocking how narrow the achievement is. The specificity makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Are you sad that Dwight beat you? No. Are you gonna cry, Jim? Do you need a tissue?
jokes.setup: Interviewer asking if Jim is upset about losing to Dwight
jokes.punchline: The increasingly mocking questions reveal the interviewer is clearly Dwight off-camera
jokes.explanation: Clever reveal that Dwight is conducting his own interview. The escalating mockery makes it obvious while Jim plays along deadpan. Great use of documentary format.

jokes.text: I don't want to offend... Angela. Or someone.
jokes.setup: Pam explaining why wedding planning at work is awkward
jokes.punchline: Her pause before saying 'Angela' makes it clear she specifically means Angela, then tries to cover
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Pam's politeness prevents her from directly naming Angela, but the pause gives it away. Shows office dynamics.

jokes.text: That's what she said. Ha! I don't get it.
jokes.setup: Michael makes his signature joke
jokes.punchline: Dwight doesn't understand the sexual innuendo
jokes.explanation: Standard 'That's what she said' but Dwight's confusion adds a layer. Not original but character-appropriate.

jokes.text: Grapes, seductive. ... Oh, absofruitly. Fruit, grapes. Nailed the joke. Matter of time.
jokes.setup: Dwight is trying to understand innuendo through fruit analogies
jokes.punchline: His forced fruit puns and confidence that he 'nailed the joke' when he clearly didn't
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight - his literal mind trying to process humor through categories, complete confidence in his failure. 'Absofruitly' is wonderfully awkward.

jokes.text: Been there, done that. Went there again, did it again. Two years in a row. Consecutive.
jokes.setup: Michael downplaying the importance of the speaking engagement
jokes.punchline: He clarifies 'two years in a row' with 'consecutive' as if that wasn't obvious
jokes.explanation: Michael's need to over-explain obvious things. The redundant clarification of 'consecutive' after 'two years in a row' is perfectly him.

jokes.text: They stopped making plaques that year.
jokes.setup: Michael explaining why he got a certificate instead of a plaque
jokes.punchline: He delivers this obviously made-up explanation with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: Michael's inability to admit he might have performed worse. The matter-of-fact delivery of an absurd explanation is classic Michael.

jokes.text: I misspelled, in front of the entire school, the word 'failure'.
jokes.setup: Michael recounting his childhood spelling bee trauma
jokes.punchline: The word he failed to spell was literally 'failure'
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic setup - of course Michael would fail at spelling the word 'failure.' The cosmic irony is beautifully constructed and emotionally resonant.

jokes.text: That's because you're incapable of doing it. Because you don't know how. Because you have no skills.
jokes.setup: Dwight says he can't give the speech
jokes.punchline: Michael's 'pep talk' is just brutal honesty about Dwight's incompetence
jokes.explanation: Michael's version of motivation is just listing Dwight's flaws. The complete lack of encouragement while trying to help is peak Michael.

jokes.text: Is that an insult or is that part of the public speaking advice?
jokes.setup: Michael calls Dwight an idiot
jokes.punchline: Dwight genuinely can't tell if it's coaching or an insult
jokes.explanation: Dwight's literal mind can't distinguish between Michael's abuse and instruction. Perfect character moment showing their dynamic.

jokes.text: I get here early every morning so I can set the thermostat. I like it a little cooler, around 66 degrees. I'm more productive. Maybe some people don't like it as cold as I do. But I don't care.
jokes.setup: Angela explaining her morning routine
jokes.punchline: Her fake consideration for others followed by 'But I don't care' reveals her true nature
jokes.explanation: Perfect Angela - the veneer of reasonableness stripped away to reveal her controlling nature. The fake empathy makes it funnier.

jokes.text: A saleswoman has a vagina.
jokes.setup: Michael asks about the difference between a salesman and saleswoman
jokes.punchline: Dwight gives the most literal, inappropriate answer possible
jokes.explanation: Dwight's literal mind producing maximum awkwardness. While crude, it fits his character's inability to understand social context.

jokes.text: The difference between a salesman and a saleswoman is boobs.
jokes.setup: Michael correcting Dwight's joke
jokes.punchline: His 'better' punchline is equally inappropriate but slightly less clinical
jokes.explanation: Michael thinking his version is more sophisticated. The marginal improvement from 'vagina' to 'boobs' shows his juvenile sense of humor.

jokes.text: I have some very great news from corporate. We had a wonderful quarter, and as a result, all of you are getting bonuses for $1,000.
jokes.setup: Michael announces great news about bonuses
jokes.punchline: The entire announcement is fake, just to demonstrate audience engagement
jokes.explanation: The misdirection works well - viewers and employees both get excited before the rug is pulled. Shows Michael's manipulation.

jokes.text: No no, it's not true. I was just talking.
jokes.setup: Everyone celebrates the bonus news
jokes.punchline: Michael casually reveals it was all fake with no consideration for their feelings
jokes.explanation: Michael's complete obliviousness to how cruel this is. His casual dismissal after getting their hopes up is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: There had been an accident on 84 West. Cars have skidded off the road into the safety railing. Several cars have flipped. There is broken glass everywhere. Several people are injured.
jokes.setup: Dwight trying to copy Michael's demonstration technique
jokes.punchline: He creates an elaborate fake disaster scenario that's completely inappropriate
jokes.explanation: Dwight's literal mind takes Michael's lesson and creates something darker and more detailed. The escalating disaster details show his intensity.

jokes.text: Brad Pitt. ... He will never act again. Also, this branch is closing.
jokes.setup: People ask who was in the accident
jokes.punchline: Dwight names Brad Pitt then adds their branch is closing for no logical reason
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd escalation - from fake accident to celebrity victim to branch closing. Dwight's complete disconnect from logic is perfect.

jokes.text: This is karma because of what he did to Jennifer Anniston.
jokes.setup: Dwight announces Brad Pitt will never act again
jokes.punchline: Kevin connects this to Brad Pitt's personal life drama
jokes.explanation: Kevin's simple mind connecting disaster to celebrity gossip. Pop culture reference but fits his character's interests.

jokes.text: Pam, I'm public speaking. Stop public interrupting me.
jokes.setup: Pam interrupts Michael during his toastmasters explanation
jokes.punchline: Michael's awkward wordplay turning 'interrupting' into 'public interrupting'
jokes.explanation: Michael's forced wordplay shows his need to always seem clever. The awkward construction makes it funnier than if it worked smoothly.

jokes.text: What is that? It's like Club Med, but everything is naked.
jokes.setup: Jim asks about Hedonism resort recommendation
jokes.punchline: The matter-of-fact explanation of a sex resort as vacation advice
jokes.explanation: The casual description of a nude resort as vacation advice. The deadpan delivery makes it funnier than if it were played for shock.

jokes.text: Maybe a month.
jokes.setup: Stanley says he went to Amsterdam for about a week
jokes.punchline: He corrects himself to maybe a month, implying he lost track of time
jokes.explanation: Stanley's casual admission that his Amsterdam trip lasted much longer than planned. Implies he was having too much fun to keep track.

jokes.text: I'd like to say hi to my friends in China.
jokes.setup: Michael suggests sending Jim to Hong Kong
jokes.punchline: Creed's random interjection about saying hi to friends in China
jokes.explanation: Classic Creed randomness - he somehow has friends in China and thinks this is relevant. His non-sequiturs are always character-appropriate.

jokes.text: Good morning, Vietnam!
jokes.setup: Dwight is asked to show what he learned about public speaking
jokes.punchline: He just shouts the Robin Williams movie line
jokes.explanation: Pure pop culture reference without any twist. Works for Dwight's character but lacks originality. His confidence in this weak choice is the real joke.

jokes.text: Which means absolutely nothing. ... Well, it's mostly made up.
jokes.setup: Dwight claims authority as assistant regional manager
jokes.punchline: Jim and Michael immediately deflate his claim
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the running joke about Dwight's made-up title. Michael's casual admission that it's 'mostly made up' is perfectly dismissive.

jokes.text: I can travel anywhere, except Cuba. And I will travel to New Zealand. And walk the Lord of the Rings trail to Mordor. And then I will hike Mt. Doom.
jokes.setup: Asked where he'd travel
jokes.punchline: Dwight's oddly specific restriction about Cuba followed by elaborate LOTR tourism plans
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight - the random Cuba restriction plus his intense LOTR fandom. The specificity of his fantasy trip is character-perfect.

jokes.text: Please, you're not taking any trip.
jokes.setup: Jim asking for travel advice
jokes.punchline: Dwight brutally dismisses Jim's travel plans
jokes.explanation: Dwight's blunt assessment that Jim won't follow through. Shows their rivalry and Dwight's directness.

jokes.text: You've gotta wave your arms, and you've gotta pound your fists. Many times. So as to emphasize your point.
jokes.setup: Jim giving Dwight public speaking advice about great speakers
jokes.punchline: His advice sounds like he's describing dictators, not inspirational speakers
jokes.explanation: Jim's sabotage is subtle - the advice sounds legitimate until you realize he's describing fascist rallies. Sets up the Mussolini reveal.

jokes.text: But I did download speeches from some of history's famous dictators. Like this one. Originally given by Benito Mussolini.
jokes.setup: Jim admits he didn't major in public speaking
jokes.punchline: He reveals he's been giving Dwight fascist speech advice
jokes.explanation: Brilliant reveal of Jim's elaborate prank. The casual admission that he's training Dwight with fascist propaganda is darkly hilarious and perfectly planned.

jokes.text: You're inviting Jim? Of course, he's one of my closest friends.
jokes.setup: Roy questions inviting Jim to their wedding
jokes.punchline: Pam's response and Roy's reaction reveal the uncomfortable dynamic
jokes.explanation: The tension in this exchange reveals Roy's jealousy and Pam's defensive response. Good character development disguised as casual conversation.

jokes.text: I am just feeling under the weather. And I think that I will go home and rest.
jokes.setup: Dwight facing the large audience
jokes.punchline: He gets stage fright and tries to escape with an obvious lie
jokes.explanation: Dwight's stage fright overwhelming his usual confidence. The transparent excuse shows his desperation to escape.

jokes.text: I've never ever seen you take a sick day. Well, I've seen you take enough for the both of us.
jokes.setup: Angela noting Dwight never takes sick days
jokes.punchline: Dwight's cutting response about Angela's frequent absences
jokes.explanation: Good comeback from Dwight despite his panic. Shows their relationship dynamic and his defensive streak.

jokes.text: Well, there are many things, I believe, that do such a thing of that... nature.
jokes.setup: Michael trying to explain what makes excellence
jokes.punchline: He gets completely lost in circular, meaningless language
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael - trying to sound profound but producing complete nonsense. The tortured syntax shows his panic at having nothing to say.

jokes.text: I always set it at 69.
jokes.setup: Discussion of thermostat temperatures
jokes.punchline: Toby's innocent comment has sexual connotations
jokes.explanation: Juvenile humor but fits the show's style. Toby's obliviousness to the innuendo makes it character-appropriate.

jokes.text: Actually, I don't see ever getting married.
jokes.setup: Wedding discussion with engaged Pam
jokes.punchline: Ryan's blunt dismissal of marriage in front of someone planning a wedding
jokes.explanation: Ryan's tactless honesty creating awkwardness. Shows his self-centered nature and social cluelessness.

jokes.text: Ryan, you should be more sensitive. It's obvious she likes you, and comments like that... I know what I said.
jokes.setup: Kelly trying to make Ryan be more sensitive
jokes.punchline: Ryan cuts her off, showing he doesn't care about being insensitive
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Ryan's callousness and Kelly's attempt to manage his behavior. His dismissive response is perfectly him.

jokes.text: I'm very sorry, I did not know that you were wearing a hearing aid. And I just thought you were speaking abnormally.
jokes.setup: Michael addressing someone in the audience
jokes.punchline: His apology is somehow more offensive than whatever he originally said
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael - his attempt to be sensitive makes things worse. The 'speaking abnormally' comment is perfectly tone-deaf.

jokes.text: Michael Winslow. Anyone? A car starting.
jokes.setup: Michael trying to do impressions for the audience
jokes.punchline: His reference is obscure and his impression presumably terrible
jokes.explanation: Michael's desperate attempt to connect with the audience through dated pop culture. His failure to land the reference is character-appropriate.

jokes.text: Blood alone moves the wheels of history!
jokes.setup: Dwight finally takes the stage
jokes.punchline: He opens with Mussolini's fascist rhetoric
jokes.explanation: The payoff to Jim's elaborate prank. Dwight delivering fascist propaganda with complete conviction is both horrifying and hilarious. Perfect escalation.

jokes.text: We realized that the world could be conquered. It has been a lifetime struggle. A never-ending fight. I say to you, and you will understand, that it is a privilege to fight! We are warriors!
jokes.setup: Dwight continuing the fascist speech
jokes.punchline: He's getting increasingly passionate about conquering the world
jokes.explanation: Dwight's intensity makes the fascist rhetoric both terrifying and absurd. His natural authoritarianism fits perfectly with the content.

jokes.text: Jim has worked at the same place for five years. Jim eats the same ham and cheese sandwich every day for lunch. I don't know, if I were a betting man, I'd say he will have a fun weekend in Philadelphia.
jokes.setup: Jim talking about his travel plans
jokes.punchline: Dwight's analysis reduces Jim's adventurous plans to a predictable weekend trip
jokes.explanation: Dwight's brutal but accurate assessment of Jim's predictable nature. The specific observations make it sting more than a generic insult.

jokes.text: Salesmen... and women... of the world, unite. We must never acquiesce. For it is together! Together that we prevail!
jokes.setup: Dwight adapting fascist rhetoric to sales
jokes.punchline: He's calling for salespeople to unite using communist/fascist language
jokes.explanation: Brilliant adaptation of fascist rhetoric to office sales culture. The political language applied to paper sales is absurdist perfection.

jokes.text: I'm a little nervous to run into Dwight on his connecting flight to Mordor, but other than that...
jokes.setup: Jim discussing his Australia trip
jokes.punchline: Casual reference to Dwight's earlier LOTR travel fantasy
jokes.explanation: Nice callback to Dwight's LOTR obsession. Jim's deadpan delivery makes the absurd reference feel natural.

jokes.text: I'm leaving on June 8th. Oh. Yeah, and I'm really sorry about that.
jokes.setup: Jim reveals when he's leaving
jokes.punchline: The date conflicts with Pam's wedding, creating an uncomfortable moment
jokes.explanation: Perfectly awkward moment revealing Jim's conflict about Pam's wedding. The timing makes his avoidance clear without stating it.

jokes.text: The bartender asked for her ID, which I thought was odd. Because I pegged her as, like, 35.
jokes.setup: Michael telling his bar story
jokes.punchline: He thought carding a 35-year-old was odd, revealing his poor judgment
jokes.explanation: Michael's obliviousness to why someone who looks 35 would be carded. Shows his lack of understanding about people and situations.

jokes.text: She's probably in the room drinking from the minibar.
jokes.setup: Woman left to get her ID and never returned
jokes.punchline: Michael's explanation for why she didn't come back
jokes.explanation: Michael's need to create elaborate explanations for simple situations. His fantasy that she's still around shows his denial.

jokes.text: So I captivated the guy who captivated 1,000 guys. Can you believe that? 1,000 guys.
jokes.setup: Michael describing entertaining Dwight
jokes.punchline: His convoluted logic about captivating someone who captivated others, with emphasis on the number
jokes.explanation: Michael's desperate attempt to claim credit for Dwight's success. The transitive property of entertainment is perfectly Michael logic.
